name: France
summary: France is central to Level 3 study and spans maritime, continental and Mediterranean influences.

bordeaux.name: Bordeaux
bordeaux.summary: Classic maritime French region known for structured red blends and important dry and sweet whites.
bordeaux.styleSummary: Structured reds, fresh acidity, and important dry and sweet whites.
examTips: Think blends rather than single-variety wines.
Maritime climate means vintage variation matters.
Left Bank and Right Bank styles are a key exam theme.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Structured late-ripening grape bringing tannin, black fruit and backbone to Bordeaux blends.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Blackcurrant, cedar, firm tannin, structured reds.
examTips: High tannin and blackcurrant are key clues.
Especially important in Left Bank blends.

Merlot.summary: Earlier-ripening grape giving plum fruit and softer texture to Bordeaux blends.
Merlot.style: Plum, black fruit, softer tannin, rounder texture.
examTips: Softer and rounder than Cabernet Sauvignon.
Especially important in Right Bank blends.

Cabernet Franc.summary: Fresh red-fruited grape adding lift and structure in blends.
Cabernet Franc.style: Red fruit, herbal notes, freshness and structure in blends.
examTips: Often contributes freshness and aromatics in blends.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: High-acid aromatic white grape used in fresh dry Bordeaux white blends.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Citrus and herb notes in dry whites.
viticulture: Can lose intensity in hot sites
examTips: Think freshness and aromatic lift in dry white blends.

Semillon.summary: Waxy-textured white grape important in both dry blends and sweet botrytised wines.
Semillon.style: Waxy lemon and stone fruit; also important in sweet botrytised wines.
examTips: Key grape for sweet Bordeaux.

south-west-france.name: South West France
south-west-france.summary: Diverse inland and Atlantic-influenced region producing structured reds, aromatic whites and sweet wines.
south-west-france.styleSummary: Diverse region with structured reds, aromatic whites and sweet wines.
examTips: Think diversity rather than one signature style.
Malbec and Tannat are important for structured reds.

Malbec.summary: Deep-coloured grape producing dark-fruited, structured wines with firm tannin.
Malbec.style: Deep colour, dark fruit and firm tannin.
examTips: Think deep colour and firm tannin.
Usually fuller and more structured than lighter red styles.

Tannat.summary: Very tannic dark-fruited grape capable of powerful, deeply coloured wines.
Tannat.style: Very deeply coloured, tannic reds with dark fruit.
winemaking: Can need oak or time to soften
examTips: High tannin is the biggest clue.
Often needs ageing or careful winemaking to soften.

Petit Manseng.summary: Aromatic white grape capable of rich dry and sweet wines while retaining high acidity.
Petit Manseng.style: Aromatic rich whites, often with high acidity and sweet styles possible.
examTips: High acidity plus richness is a useful clue.

burgundy.name: Burgundy
burgundy.summary: Classic continental region focused on Pinot Noir and Chardonnay with strong site expression.
burgundy.styleSummary: Perfumed Pinot Noir and precise Chardonnay with strong site expression.
examTips: Site and vineyard position are central to Burgundy.
Burgundy is mainly Pinot Noir for reds and Chardonnay for whites.

Pinot Noir.summary: Thin-skinned red grape producing perfumed, high-acid wines with red fruit and earthy notes.
Pinot Noir.style: Red cherry, raspberry, floral and earthy notes with fresh acidity.
viticulture: Sensitive to site and rot pressure
examTips: High acidity with red fruit and lighter body is a key clue.
Very site-sensitive.

Chardonnay.summary: Versatile white grape producing styles from lean mineral wines to richer oaked examples.
Chardonnay.style: From lean mineral styles to richer nutty and oaky styles depending on site.
examTips: Think acidity plus site variation.
MLF and oak can strongly affect style.

chablis.name: Chablis
chablis.summary: Cool continental Chardonnay region known for lean, high-acid wines with citrus and mineral character.
chablis.styleSummary: Lean, high-acid Chardonnay with citrus and mineral profile.
examTips: Chablis is all about cool-climate Chardonnay.
High acidity and restrained oak are key clues.

Chardonnay.summary: Cool-climate Chardonnay showing lemon, green apple and mineral freshness, often with little or no oak.
Chardonnay.style: Green apple, lemon, very high acidity, often unoaked or subtly oaked.
examTips: Think leaner and more mineral than richer Burgundy Chardonnay.
High acidity is essential.

cote-dor.name: Cote d'Or
cote-dor.summary: The heartland of premium Burgundy, known for precise site expression from Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
cote-dor.styleSummary: The classic heartland of premium Burgundy with strong site hierarchy.
examTips: Site hierarchy and mid-slope vineyard position are key concepts.
Premium Burgundy is strongly linked to Cote d'Or.

Pinot Noir.summary: Fine, site-sensitive red grape producing elegant red-fruited wines with freshness and savoury complexity.
Pinot Noir.style: Elegant red fruit, floral perfume, savoury complexity and fresh acid.
examTips: Think perfume, freshness and fine structure.
Site expression matters more than heavy winemaking.

Chardonnay.summary: Premium white grape giving complex, concentrated wines with citrus, stone fruit, nut and oak notes.
Chardonnay.style: Complex, concentrated white styles with citrus, stone fruit, nut and oak notes.
examTips: Concentration plus freshness is key.
Often richer and more layered than Chablis.

maconnais.name: Maconnais
maconnais.summary: Warmer southern Burgundy area producing fruitier, riper Chardonnay than northern Burgundy.
maconnais.styleSummary: Riper, fruitier Chardonnay than northern Burgundy.
examTips: Think warmer, fruitier and less austere than Chablis.
Still Burgundy Chardonnay, but usually at a more approachable style.

Chardonnay.summary: Riper-fruited Chardonnay with citrus and stone fruit, usually less austere than Chablis.
Chardonnay.style: Stone fruit, citrus, moderate to high acidity, often less austere than Chablis.
examTips: Usually fruitier and broader than Chablis.
Warm southerly position is a key clue.

beaujolais.name: Beaujolais
beaujolais.summary: Region south of Burgundy known for fresh, fruit-driven Gamay with low tannin and bright acidity.
beaujolais.styleSummary: Fresh, fruit-driven reds, often lighter-bodied, from Gamay.
examTips: Think juicy red fruit, low tannin and freshness.
Semi-carbonic maceration is a key production clue.

Gamay.summary: Light to medium-bodied red grape producing juicy red fruit, fresh acidity and low tannin.
Gamay.style: Juicy red fruit, fresh acidity and low tannin.
viticulture: Can ripen well in cooler areas
examTips: Low tannin and juicy fruit are key clues.
Banana/pear-drop notes may suggest carbonic influence.

alsace.name: Alsace
alsace.summary: Dry continental region in eastern France known for aromatic whites and a relatively dry climate due to the Vosges rain shadow.
alsace.styleSummary: Aromatic whites in a relatively dry climate due to rain shadow.
examTips: Alsace is strongly associated with aromatic varietal whites.
Dry climate from rain shadow is a key regional clue.

Riesling.summary: High-acid aromatic white grape producing dry citrus and stone-fruit styles with mineral freshness.
Riesling.style: Dry, high-acid, citrus and stone-fruit white.
examTips: Think dry style plus high acidity.
Often more textural and fuller than German Kabinett styles.

Pinot Gris.summary: Richer, broader aromatic white grape often giving spice and stone-fruit notes.
Pinot Gris.style: Richer, broader white with stone fruit and spice.
examTips: Usually broader and richer than Riesling.
Watch for off-dry examples.

Gewurztraminer.summary: Highly aromatic grape producing full-bodied wines with floral, lychee and spice notes.
Gewurztraminer.style: Highly aromatic, floral, lychee and spice notes.
viticulture: Aromatic grape suited to warm sunny sites
examTips: Very aromatic, lower-acid feel and fuller body are key clues.

Muscat.summary: Aromatic variety producing floral, grapey whites with freshness.
Muscat.style: Floral, grapey aromatic white with freshness.
examTips: Grapey aroma is the classic clue.

muscadet.name: Muscadet
muscadet.summary: Cool maritime Loire region producing light, crisp whites with saline freshness, often enhanced by lees ageing.
muscadet.styleSummary: Light, crisp whites often with saline freshness.
examTips: Think light body, high acidity and possible lees texture.
Classic pairing with shellfish.

Melon Blanc.summary: Light-bodied high-acid white grape producing crisp citrus-led wines, often with lees-derived texture.
examTips: High acidity plus light body is key.
Lees ageing can add subtle texture without obvious oak.

anjou-saumur.name: Anjou-Saumur
anjou-saumur.summary: Important Loire subregion known for Chenin Blanc in dry, sweet and sparkling styles, plus Cabernet Franc reds.
anjou-saumur.styleSummary: Dry and sweet Chenin Blanc plus reds and sparkling wines.
examTips: Chenin Blanc is the key grape here.
Think versatility: dry, sweet and sparkling.

Chenin Blanc.summary: Very versatile high-acid grape capable of dry, off-dry, sweet and sparkling styles.
Chenin Blanc.style: Can range from dry to sweet; high acidity with apple, quince and honey notes.
winemaking: Dry, off-dry, sweet and sparkling styles possible
examTips: High acidity is the key clue.
Style can vary hugely depending on sweetness and winemaking.

Cabernet Franc.summary: Fresh red-fruited grape with herbal notes and moderate tannin, important in Loire reds.
Cabernet Franc.style: Red fruit, herbal notes and medium tannin.
examTips: Think fresher and lighter than many Bordeaux reds.

touraine.name: Touraine
touraine.summary: Loire region producing fresh Sauvignon Blanc and lighter Cabernet Franc-based reds with stronger inland influence.
touraine.styleSummary: Fresh whites and lighter reds with more inland influence than western Loire.
examTips: Think fresh Loire Sauvignon Blanc and lighter reds.
More inland influence than maritime western Loire.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: Fresh aromatic white grape with high acidity and citrus-led profile.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Fresh citrus and green fruit with high acidity.
examTips: High acidity and fresh herbal/citrus profile are strong clues.

Cabernet Franc.summary: Medium-bodied red grape producing fresh red fruit and herbal notes.
Cabernet Franc.style: Fresh red fruit and herbal notes.
examTips: Usually fresher and lighter than Bordeaux styles.

central-vineyards.name: Central Vineyards
central-vineyards.summary: Cool inland Loire area focused on precise, high-acid Sauvignon Blanc wines.
central-vineyards.styleSummary: Focused on high-acid Sauvignon Blanc styles.
examTips: This is a key Loire Sauvignon Blanc zone.
Think high acidity, citrus and herbal freshness.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: High-acid aromatic grape producing citrus, gooseberry and herbal notes in cool climates.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: High acidity, citrus, gooseberry and herbal notes.
viticulture: Aromatic grape suited to cool conditions
winemaking: Usually stainless steel and fresh style
examTips: Very high freshness and clean aromatic profile are key clues.

northern-rhone.name: Northern Rhone
northern-rhone.summary: Steep continental valley region known for structured Syrah and aromatic Viognier.
northern-rhone.styleSummary: Structured Syrah reds and aromatic Viognier whites.
examTips: Syrah is the key red grape here.
Think pepper, violet and savoury spice for classic Northern Rhone Syrah.

Syrah.summary: Classic Northern Rhone red grape giving black fruit, pepper, violet and savoury spice.
Syrah.style: Black fruit, pepper, violet and savoury spice.
viticulture: Needs warmth but not excessive heat
examTips: Pepper and violet are key exam clues.
Usually more savoury and structured than many warm-climate Shiraz styles.

Viognier.summary: Aromatic full-bodied white grape with apricot, peach and floral character.
Viognier.style: Aromatic apricot, peach and floral white with full body.
viticulture: Needs careful handling to preserve aromatics
examTips: Low to medium acidity but pronounced aroma is a useful clue.

southern-rhone.name: Southern Rhone
southern-rhone.summary: Warm Mediterranean region known for Grenache-led blends with ripe fruit, body and higher alcohol.
southern-rhone.styleSummary: Ripe fruit, fuller body and higher alcohol in Grenache-led blends.
examTips: Think GSM-style blends and warm ripe fruit.
Grenache is usually the dominant grape.

Grenache.summary: Warm-climate grape giving ripe red fruit, spice, body and high alcohol.
Grenache.style: Ripe red fruit, spice, high alcohol and softer tannin.
examTips: High alcohol and ripe red fruit are key clues.
Usually softer in tannin than Syrah.

Syrah.summary: Blending grape adding black fruit, pepper, colour and structure.
Syrah.style: Black fruit, pepper and added colour/structure in blends.
examTips: Usually provides structure and darker fruit in blends.

Mourvedre.summary: Late-ripening grape adding dark fruit, meaty character and firm tannin to blends.
Mourvedre.style: Dark fruit, meaty and spicy notes with firm tannin.
viticulture: Needs lots of heat to ripen fully
examTips: Needs heat and usually contributes structure.

languedoc-roussillon.name: Languedoc-Roussillon
languedoc-roussillon.summary: Large warm Mediterranean region producing ripe reds, fresh whites and a wide range of styles from varied sites.
languedoc-roussillon.styleSummary: Ripe fruit, generous body and wide stylistic diversity.
examTips: Think warm Mediterranean ripeness and regional diversity.
Old vines and blending are important themes.

Carignan.summary: Dark-fruited grape often used in blends, capable of rustic tannin and concentration, especially from old vines.
Carignan.style: Dark fruit and rustic tannin, often used in blends.
viticulture: Can crop heavily if not controlled
examTips: Old vines are often associated with better quality.
Think structure more than perfume.

Cinsault.summary: Soft-fruited warm-climate grape often used to add freshness and softness to blends.
Cinsault.style: Soft red fruit and lower tannin, useful for blending.
examTips: Usually softer and lighter in structure than Carignan.

Picpoul.summary: High-acid white grape producing fresh citrus-led wines in Mediterranean coastal areas.
examTips: Think high acidity in a warm region because of coastal influence.

provence.name: Provence
provence.summary: Warm Mediterranean region strongly associated with pale dry rosé, plus some reds.
provence.styleSummary: Warm-climate rosé and red wines with ripe fruit profile.
examTips: Provence is primarily a rosé region in exam context.
Think pale colour, dry style and ripe but fresh fruit.

Grenache.summary: Warm-climate grape contributing ripe red fruit, body and alcohol, especially useful in rosé blends.
Grenache.style: Ripe red fruit and body, often important in rosé blends.
winemaking: Often used in rosé and red blends
examTips: Important in Provence rosé blends.

Mourvedre.summary: Late-ripening grape adding dark fruit, spice and structure in warm coastal blends.
examTips: Usually gives more structure than Grenache.

champagne.name: Champagne
champagne.summary: Cool-climate traditional method sparkling region known for high acidity, autolytic complexity and blending skill.
champagne.styleSummary: High-acid traditional method sparkling wines ranging from fresh non-vintage blends to complex prestige cuvees.
examTips: Acidity is naturally high because Champagne is a cool climate region.
Blending, second fermentation in bottle and lees ageing are core exam points.
Know the difference between non-vintage, vintage, blanc de blancs and rosé styles.

Chardonnay.summary: Important Champagne grape bringing finesse, citrus fruit and high acidity, especially in blanc de blancs styles.
Chardonnay.style: Lemon, green apple, finesse, high acidity and elegant autolytic development.
viticulture: Early budding can increase frost risk
Retains acidity well in cool climate
examTips: Often gives the finest and most elegant styles.
Blanc de Blancs = Chardonnay only.

Pinot Noir.summary: Black grape adding body, structure and red-fruit depth to Champagne blends.
Pinot Noir.style: Body, structure, red fruit and power in sparkling blends.
viticulture: Early ripening helps in cool climate
Sensitive to frost and rot pressure
examTips: Adds body and structure more than Chardonnay.
Blanc de Noirs can be made from Pinot Noir and/or Pinot Meunier.

Pinot Meunier.summary: Black grape valued for fruitiness and reliable performance in cooler, frost-prone sites.
Pinot Meunier.style: Fruitier, softer and earlier drinking than the most structured Champagne components.
viticulture: Useful in cooler or frost-prone sites
examTips: Often contributes early-drinking fruitiness.
Important supporting grape in many non-vintage blends.

champagne-nv.name: Champagne NV
champagne-nv.style: Fresh, consistent house style built from reserve wines and multi-vintage blending.
champagne-nv.aging: Minimum lees ageing for non-vintage traditional method sparkling wine

champagne-vintage.name: Champagne Vintage
champagne-vintage.style: Riper fruit, more concentration and longer ageing potential from one harvest.
champagne-vintage.keyPoint: Made only in stronger years from a single vintage.

blanc-de-blancs.name: Blanc de Blancs
blanc-de-blancs.style: Citrus-led, elegant, fine and high-acid sparkling wine.
blanc-de-blancs.aging: Traditional method, often with extended lees ageing
blanc-de-blancs.keyPoint: Made from white grapes only, usually Chardonnay.

blanc-de-noirs.name: Blanc de Noirs
blanc-de-noirs.style: Broader, more structured sparkling wine with more body.

rose-champagne.name: Rose Champagne
rose-champagne.style: Red-fruit sparkling style made by blending still red wine or short skin contact.
rose-champagne.keyPoint: Know both allowed methods for making rosé Champagne.

prestige-cuvee.name: Prestige Cuvee
prestige-cuvee.style: Top-tier complex Champagne with concentration, finesse and long ageing potential.
prestige-cuvee.aging: Extended lees ageing and bottle maturation
prestige-cuvee.keyPoint: Flagship wine of a house or grower.

examTips: Study France by climate, grape specialisation and classic regional styles.
Champagne is a core sparkling topic alongside the key still-wine regions.
Bordeaux, Burgundy, Loire, Rhone and Champagne should all be easy to compare.
